docs.title: 🌡️ Urban Heat Island Analysis in Spain
docs.text: This project investigates the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect across several Spanish cities by combining meteorological data with land use information. The UHI phenomenon refers to the temperature difference between urban and rural environments, typically caused by human activity, artificial surfaces, and reduced vegetation.

docs.title: 📌 Objectives
docs.text:
The main objectives of this project are:

  • To compute the Urban Heat Island (UHI) intensity using hourly temperature data
  • To compare UHI behaviour during daytime and nighttime
  • To analyse the relationship between land use and temperature differences
  • To evaluate the impact of urbanization and vegetation on UHI
  • To build statistical models that explain UHI variability across cities

docs.title: 🌍 Study Area
docs.text:
The analysis focuses on a selection of Spanish cities representing different climatic and geographical conditions, including:

  • Madrid
  • Barcelona
  • Sevilla
  • Zaragoza
  • Bilbao
  • A Coruña
  • Santander
  • Tenerife

This diversity allows for a more comprehensive understanding of how UHI behaves under different environmental conditions.

docs.title: 🗂️ Project Structure
docs.text:
The repository is organised as follows:

  • src/: Core implementation of the project, including data processing, UHI computation, modeling, and visualization
  • tests/: Unit tests ensuring correctness and robustness of the code
  • docs/: Project documentation built using MkDocs
  • data/: Data files used in the analysis

docs.title: ⚙️ Technologies Used
docs.text:
This project relies on several modern Python tools:

  • Pandas / Polars for data manipulation
  • GeoPandas for spatial analysis
  • Meteostat for meteorological data retrieval
  • Scikit-learn for regression modeling
  • Matplotlib / Seaborn for visualization

docs.title: 📊 Key Insights
docs.text:
Preliminary results suggest that:

  • UHI is consistently positive across cities
  • The effect is stronger during nighttime due to heat retention
  • Urban areas significantly increase local temperatures
  • Vegetation plays a mitigating role in reducing UHI intensity

docs.title: 📚 Documentation
docs.text:
For a detailed explanation of the methodology and results:

  • 👉 Methodology
  • 👉 Analysis
  • 👉 Results

docs.title: 🌡️ UHI Computation
docs.text:
The Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect is defined as the temperature difference between urban and rural environments:

UHI = T_urban − T_rural

Using hourly temperature data from Meteostat, UHI values were computed for each city over the study period. The resulting time series allow for both temporal and statistical analysis.

docs.title: Observations
docs.text:
• UHI values are generally positive, confirming the presence of the phenomenon
  • The magnitude varies depending on the city and climatic conditions
  • Seasonal patterns are observed, with stronger effects in summer months

docs.title: 🌙 Day vs Night Behaviour
docs.text: A key aspect of the analysis is the distinction between daytime and nighttime UHI.

docs.title: Findings
docs.text:
• Nighttime UHI is consistently higher than daytime UHI
  • This is due to:
  • Heat storage in urban materials (asphalt, concrete)
  • Reduced radiative cooling
  • Lower wind speeds and atmospheric mixing

docs.title: Implications
docs.text: This suggests that urban areas retain heat more efficiently than rural areas, particularly after sunset, which has important consequences for energy consumption and human comfort.

docs.title: 🌍 Land Use Analysis
docs.text: Land use data from the CORINE Land Cover dataset was used to quantify the composition of the area surrounding each meteorological station.

docs.title: Method
docs.text:
• Buffers of 5 km were created around each station
  • Spatial intersections with CORINE polygons were computed
  • Land use categories were aggregated into:
  • Urban
  • Vegetation
  • Agricultural
  • Other

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
• Cities with higher percentages of urban land show stronger UHI effects
  • Vegetation has a clear cooling effect
  • Agricultural areas show intermediate behaviour

docs.title: 📈 Correlation Analysis
docs.text: Correlation matrices were computed to explore relationships between variables.

docs.title: Key relationships
docs.text:
• Positive correlation between % urban and UHI
  • Negative correlation between % vegetation and UHI
  • Population is often correlated with urbanization

docs.title: 🧠 Interpretation
docs.text:
The results confirm well-known physical mechanisms:

  • Urban materials store and slowly release heat
  • Lack of vegetation reduces evapotranspiration
  • Urban geometry (buildings) affects airflow and radiation

Overall, the analysis supports the hypothesis that land use is a major driver of UHI intensity.

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text: Two main data sources were used:

docs.title: Meteorological Data
docs.text:
• Source: Meteostat
  • Type: Hourly temperature data
  • Variables: Temperature (°C)
  • Stations: Urban and rural locations for each city

docs.title: Land Use Data
docs.text:
• Source: CORINE Land Cover
  • Resolution: European-scale land classification
  • Categories: Urban, vegetation, agriculture, etc.

docs.title: Data Preprocessing
docs.text:
The raw data underwent several preprocessing steps:

  • Removal of missing values (NaNs)
  • Interpolation of small gaps
  • Outlier detection and filtering
  • Conversion to efficient data structures (Polars)

This ensures consistency and reliability of the analysis.

docs.title: UHI Calculation
docs.text:
The UHI was computed as:

UHI = T_urban − T_rural

For each city:

  • Hourly UHI values were calculated
  • Aggregated metrics were derived:
  • Mean UHI
  • Daytime UHI
  • Nighttime UHI

docs.title: Spatial Analysis
docs.text:
To analyse land use:

  1. Buffers of 5 km were created around each station
  2. These buffers were intersected with CORINE polygons
  3. The proportion of each land use type was computed

This provides a quantitative measure of the environment surrounding each station.

docs.title: Feature Engineering
docs.text:
The final dataset includes:

  • UHI metrics
  • % urban land
  • % vegetation
  • Population
  • Altitude difference
  • Geographic coordinates
  • Mean temperature

docs.title: Modeling
docs.text: Two regression models were used:

docs.title: Linear Regression
docs.text:
• Interpretable coefficients
  • Baseline model

docs.title: Ridge and Lasso Regression
docs.text:
• Regularization to reduce overfitting
  • More stable estimates with small datasets

docs.title: Evaluation
docs.text:
Model performance was evaluated using:

  • R² score
  • Coefficient analysis

Given the limited number of cities, emphasis was placed on interpretability rather than predictive performance.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The results confirm the presence of the Urban Heat Island effect in all analysed cities, with varying intensity depending on environmental and urban factors.

docs.title: 🌡️ UHI Patterns
docs.text:
• UHI is consistently positive
  • Stronger during nighttime
  • Seasonal variability observed

docs.title: 🌍 Influence of Land Use
docs.text:
• Urban areas significantly increase UHI
  • Vegetation reduces temperature differences
  • Mixed land use leads to intermediate effects

docs.title: Linear Regression
docs.text:
• Positive coefficients for urban variables
  • Negative coefficients for vegetation

docs.title: Ridge and Lasso Regression
docs.text:
• Similar trends with improved stability
  • Reduced sensitivity to small sample size

docs.title: ⚠️ Limitations
docs.text:
• Small number of cities
  • Limited spatial resolution
  • Potential measurement errors in stations

docs.title: 🚀 Future Work
docs.text:
• Incorporate satellite data (e.g., land surface temperature)
  • Increase number of cities
  • Explore non-linear models
  • Analyse temporal dynamics in more detail
